walkthrough.title: Select and Assemble the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Kingdom Builder set up. First, find the 8 game board sections. These are double-sided boards with a landscape of hexes on the front and a gold score track on the back. Pick any 4 of the 8 sections and assemble them into a rectangle, two boards wide and two boards tall. The boards can go in any orientation you like. Each section has 100 terrain hexes on it, so your final board will be a big grid of 400 hexes.

walkthrough.title: Identify the Terrain Types
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to look at the board. You will see 9 different terrain types. Five of them are buildable: Grass, Canyon, Desert, Flower Field, and Forest. These are where you will place your settlements. The other four are not buildable: Castle, Water, Mountain, and Location hexes. You can never build directly on those. Castles are important for end-game scoring, and Location hexes give you special power tiles when you build next to them.

walkthrough.title: Place Location Tile Summaries
walkthrough.text: Now look at your 4 board sections. Each one has a pair of Location hexes somewhere on it, marked with a special icon. Find the 4 summary cards that match the location types on your board. Place each summary card next to the board section that has the matching location hexes. These summaries remind everyone what each location tile does during the game.

walkthrough.title: Place Location Tiles on the Board
walkthrough.text: Each board section has exactly 2 location hexes of the same type. For each location hex, place one location tile face up on it. So you will place 2 tiles per board section, 8 tiles total across the whole board. These tiles are what players will pick up during the game when they build a settlement next to a location hex.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Terrain Card Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the 25 terrain cards. These are the cards that tell you which terrain type you must build on each turn. There are 5 cards for each of the 5 buildable terrain types: 5 Grass, 5 Flower Field, 5 Forest, 5 Canyon, and 5 Desert. Shuffle them all together into one face-down draw pile and place it within easy reach of all players.

walkthrough.title: Draw Kingdom Builder Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the 10 Kingdom Builder cards. These are the scoring condition cards that determine how you earn gold at the end of the game. Shuffle all 10, then randomly draw 3 and place them face up next to the board where everyone can see them. Put the remaining 7 back in the box. These 3 cards are critical because they define how you earn points this game. Every game uses a different combination, which keeps things fresh.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Player Components
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes all 40 settlements of that color. This is your personal supply for the entire game. You also get 1 gold marker in your color. Now take one of the unused game board sections, flip it over to its back side showing the gold score track, and place it next to the board. Each player places their gold marker on the black starting space of the track. This is where you will record final scores.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Terrain Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player draws 1 terrain card from the top of the draw pile and keeps it hidden from the other players. This is your starting hand. You will always have exactly 1 terrain card in hand at any time.

walkthrough.title: Determine the Start Player
walkthrough.text: Give the start player tile to the oldest player at the table. They will take the first turn. Play proceeds clockwise from the start player. Put any unused components back in the box, and you are ready to go.

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: Kingdom Builder is played over several rounds. On your turn, you do three things in this order: First, you play your terrain card face up. Second, you perform your mandatory action by building 3 settlements. Third, you may perform any extra actions from location tiles you have collected. After you are done, discard your terrain card and draw a new one. Then the next player clockwise takes their turn.

walkthrough.title: The Mandatory Action: Build 3 Settlements
walkthrough.text: When you play your terrain card, you must build exactly 3 settlements from your personal supply onto unoccupied hexes that match the terrain type shown on your card. So if you play a Forest card, all 3 settlements must go on Forest hexes. You build them one at a time, and each placement must follow the building rules. This is not optional. You must build all 3 if possible.

walkthrough.title: Building Rule 1: One Settlement Per Hex
walkthrough.text: Each hex can hold exactly one settlement. You cannot stack settlements or place on an occupied hex. Simple as that.

walkthrough.title: Building Rule 2: Buildable Terrain Only
walkthrough.text: Settlements may only be built on the five buildable terrain types: Grass, Canyon, Desert, Flower Field, and Forest. You can never build on Castle, Water, Mountain, or Location hexes. The one exception is the Harbor location tile, which lets you move a settlement onto a Water hex as a special extra action.

walkthrough.title: Building Rule 3: Build Adjacent If Possible
walkthrough.text: This is the most important rule and the one new players mess up the most. Whenever you place a settlement, you must build it adjacent to at least one of your existing settlements, if possible. Look at all unoccupied hexes of the required terrain type. If any of them are next to one of your settlements, you must choose one of those. You only get to build on a non-adjacent hex of the matching terrain if there are zero adjacent options available. This rule applies to each of the 3 settlements individually, so after placing your first settlement, check again for adjacency before placing the second.

walkthrough.title: What Happens When No Valid Hex Exists
walkthrough.text: In the rare case where there is no unoccupied hex matching your terrain card at the start of your turn or during your turn, you immediately draw a new terrain card and remove the useless one from the game entirely. If the new card also has no valid hex, keep drawing until you find one that works. This almost never happens, but it is good to know.

walkthrough.title: Extra Actions from Location Tiles
walkthrough.text: During the game, you will collect location tiles. Each tile grants you one extra action per turn. You may use each tile once per turn, and you can perform your extra actions before or after your mandatory action, or even between individual mandatory settlements. After using a tile, flip it to its picture side to show it has been used this turn. At the end of your turn, flip all your tiles back to the pictogram side so they are ready for next turn.

walkthrough.title: How to Get Location Tiles
walkthrough.text: Whenever you build a settlement adjacent to a Location hex, you immediately take one of the location tiles from that hex, if any are still available. Place it in front of you, picture side up, and you can use it starting next turn. Important details: You may only take one tile from each specific location hex. You can have 2 identical tiles only if you built next to both location hexes of that type on the same board section. If both tiles on a location hex are already taken, you get nothing.

walkthrough.title: Keeping Your Location Tiles
walkthrough.text: You keep a location tile as long as at least one of your settlements is adjacent to the corresponding location hex. If you use an extra action to move your last settlement away from that location, you must discard the tile and remove it from the game permanently. So be careful when moving settlements near locations.

walkthrough.title: Location Tile: Oracle
walkthrough.text: The Oracle lets you build one additional settlement on a hex of the same terrain type as your played terrain card. The adjacency rule applies: build adjacent to your own settlements if possible. Think of it as a bonus fourth mandatory-style placement.

walkthrough.title: Location Tile: Farm
walkthrough.text: The Farm lets you build one additional settlement on a Grass hex, regardless of what terrain card you played. Build adjacent if possible. If there are no unoccupied Grass hexes on the entire board, skip this action.

walkthrough.title: Location Tile: Oasis
walkthrough.text: The Oasis lets you build one additional settlement on a Desert hex, regardless of your terrain card. Build adjacent if possible. If there are no unoccupied Desert hexes left, skip this action.

walkthrough.title: Location Tile: Tower
walkthrough.text: The Tower lets you build one additional settlement on any suitable terrain hex at the edge of the game board. The hex must be one of the 5 buildable terrain types, and it must be on the outer rim of the board. Build adjacent if possible.

walkthrough.title: Location Tile: Tavern
walkthrough.text: The Tavern lets you build one additional settlement at one end of a straight line of at least 3 of your own settlements. The line can run in any of the hex directions, horizontally or diagonally. The hex where you build must be a buildable terrain type. This is a powerful way to extend your lines of settlements.

walkthrough.title: Location Tile: Barn
walkthrough.text: The Barn lets you move one of your existing settlements to a new hex of the same terrain type as your played terrain card. The normal adjacency rule applies: move adjacent to your own settlements if possible. This is a repositioning tool to fix poor placements.

walkthrough.title: Location Tile: Harbor
walkthrough.text: The Harbor lets you move one of your existing settlements to a Water hex. Yes, Water. This is the only way to place a settlement on Water in the entire game. Build adjacent if possible. Note: the Fishermen Kingdom Builder card does not award gold for settlements on Water hexes, even though they are next to water.

walkthrough.title: Location Tile: Paddock
walkthrough.text: The Paddock lets you move one of your existing settlements exactly 2 hexes in a straight line in any direction. You can jump over any terrain type, including Water, Mountains, Castles, Locations, and even other settlements. The landing hex must be an unoccupied buildable terrain. Crucially, the adjacency rule does not apply to the Paddock. This is the only action in the game where you can land on a non-adjacent hex freely.

walkthrough.title: End of Turn: Draw a New Card
walkthrough.text: After you have finished your mandatory action and all the extra actions you want to perform, put your played terrain card on the discard pile and draw a new card from the draw pile. Keep it hidden. If the draw pile runs out, shuffle the discard pile to form a new draw pile. Now the next player takes their turn.

walkthrough.title: End of Game Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game ends when any player places their last settlement from their personal supply. However, the current round is completed so that all players have had the same number of turns. The player to the right of the start player is the last to go. Then it is time for final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Now each player calculates their gold. Evaluate the 3 Kingdom Builder cards one at a time, scoring each player for each card. Then add 3 gold for every Castle hex that has at least one of your settlements adjacent to it. Note: you only get 3 gold per castle, even if multiple settlements surround it. Record totals on the gold score track. The player with the most gold wins. If tied, the tied players share the victory.

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with 3 example players: Red, Blue, and Green. We will use the suggested first-game setup from the rulebook: the 4 board sections with Tavern, Paddock, Oasis, and Farm locations, plus the Fishermen, Knights, and Merchants Kingdom Builder cards. Follow along with the moves even if your setup is different. Ready? Let's build some kingdoms.

walkthrough.title: Understanding This Game's Scoring
walkthrough.text: Before we start placing settlements, let's look at the 3 Kingdom Builder cards on the table. Fishermen awards 1 gold for each settlement adjacent to a Water hex. Knights awards 2 gold for each settlement in a horizontal line, with only your longest line counting. Merchants awards 4 gold for each location tile you are connected to by a chain of settlements. Plus, every player earns 3 gold per Castle hex they are adjacent to. Keep all of these in mind as we play.

walkthrough.title: Red's Turn 1: A Strong Opening
walkthrough.text: Red draws a Flower Field card. Red looks at the board and spots a cluster of Flower Field hexes near both a Location hex and a Water hex. Smart thinking. Red places their first settlement on a Flower Field hex adjacent to the Oasis location. They immediately pick up an Oasis location tile. Red then places settlements 2 and 3 on nearby Flower Field hexes, one of which is next to a Water hex. Three settlements down: Red has a location tile for next turn and a settlement scoring for Fishermen. Great start.

walkthrough.title: Why Red's Opening Was Smart
walkthrough.text: Red prioritized getting a location tile on their very first turn. That Oasis tile will let Red place an extra settlement on a Desert hex every turn from now on. Red also placed near Water for Fishermen gold, and kept their settlements touching only Flower Field terrain, which limits how many terrain types they are adjacent to. That matters because the adjacency rule will force you to build next to your existing settlements. Touching fewer terrain types means more freedom on future turns.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Turn 1: The Classic Mistake
walkthrough.text: Blue draws a Canyon card. Blue sees a big open area of Canyon hexes in the middle of the board and thinks, 'I will claim a big territory.' Blue places all 3 settlements in a row across the center of the board, far from any Location hexes, far from any Water, and far from any Castles. The settlements look impressive spread out, but they are not near anything that scores points or grants location tiles.

walkthrough.title: What Blue Did Wrong
walkthrough.text: Blue made the most common beginner mistake in Kingdom Builder: building in the middle of nowhere. Those settlements are not adjacent to any Location hexes, so Blue has no location tiles and no extra actions. They are not near Water, so no Fishermen gold. They are not near Castles, so no castle gold. And since those Canyon hexes are adjacent to multiple other terrain types, the adjacency rule is going to pull Blue right back to that same area on future turns. Blue is stuck with no special powers and poor scoring position.

walkthrough.title: Green's Turn 1: Solid Play
walkthrough.text: Green draws a Forest card. Green places their first settlement on a Forest hex next to the Farm location and picks up a Farm tile. Green then places settlements 2 and 3 on nearby Forest hexes that are also adjacent to a Castle hex. Green now has a location tile giving them an extra Grass placement each turn, plus 3 gold guaranteed from the Castle at end of game. Not as flashy as Red's triple-threat opening, but very solid.

walkthrough.title: Red's Turn 2: Using Extra Actions
walkthrough.text: Red draws a Desert card. Before placing mandatory settlements, Red decides to use the Oasis tile first. The Oasis places one settlement on a Desert hex, and Red chooses one near a second Location hex. Red picks up a Paddock tile now too. Then Red performs the mandatory action, placing 3 settlements on Desert hexes. Red has now placed 4 settlements this turn instead of 3, and has 2 location tiles. The extra actions are already compounding.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Turn 2: Digging a Deeper Hole
walkthrough.text: Blue draws a Grass card. Here is where the adjacency rule bites Blue hard. Blue's existing settlements in the center are adjacent to several Grass hexes. Because the adjacency rule says you MUST build next to your own settlements when possible, Blue is forced to place all 3 settlements on Grass hexes next to their existing cluster. Blue wanted to build toward a Location hex on the edge of the board, but they cannot. The adjacency rule is dragging Blue's kingdom into a clump.

walkthrough.title: Why Blue Is Stuck
walkthrough.text: This is the consequence of Blue's first turn mistake. By building in the center adjacent to many terrain types, Blue is now locked into building near that cluster almost every turn. The adjacency rule is mandatory. As long as there is a matching hex next to your settlements, you have to build there. Blue will spend the whole game expanding one big blob instead of spreading across the board for more scoring opportunities. This is the single biggest trap in Kingdom Builder.

walkthrough.title: Green's Turn 2: Spreading Out
walkthrough.text: Green draws a Canyon card. Green's existing settlements are adjacent to only Forest and Castle terrain, not Canyon. Because there are no Canyon hexes adjacent to Green's settlements, Green is free to build anywhere on the board where there are Canyon hexes. Green places settlements near Water on the other side of the board, scoring future Fishermen gold and getting close to another Location hex. Green also uses the Farm tile to place a bonus settlement on a Grass hex near a Castle. Five settlements this turn between the Farm tile bonus and the mandatory 3, and Green is now scoring in two separate areas of the board.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Mistake 2: Forgetting the Adjacency Rule
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Blue draws a Flower Field card. Blue gets excited and tries to place a settlement on a Flower Field hex on the far side of the board, away from their cluster. But wait, there are Flower Field hexes adjacent to Blue's existing settlements. Another player catches the mistake. Blue must build adjacent first. You cannot skip over adjacent hexes to build somewhere more convenient. The adjacency rule is not optional. Blue sighs and places next to the cluster again.

walkthrough.title: The Lesson: Adjacency Is Everything
walkthrough.text: The adjacency rule is the defining constraint of Kingdom Builder. If your settlements border many different terrain types, you will be forced to build near them over and over, creating a big clump. If your settlements border few terrain types, you will have more turns where no adjacent match exists, freeing you to start new clusters elsewhere on the board. The best players deliberately limit which terrain types their settlements touch, especially in the first few turns.

walkthrough.title: Red's Turn 3: Location Tile Combo
walkthrough.text: Red draws a Grass card. Red uses the Oasis tile to place a settlement on a Desert hex, then uses the Paddock to jump an existing settlement 2 hexes over a mountain toward a Castle. Then Red places the 3 mandatory Grass settlements. In one turn, Red placed 4 new settlements and moved 1 existing one. With 2 location tiles working together, Red is covering the board fast while Blue is still stuck in one clump.

walkthrough.title: Continue the Round
walkthrough.text: Play continues clockwise. Each player draws a terrain card, performs their mandatory 3 settlements, and uses any extra actions from location tiles. Notice how Red and Green keep pulling ahead because their location tiles give them 4 or 5 placements per turn while Blue only gets 3. Location tiles compound over the game. Getting them early means more total settlements on the board and better positioning for scoring.

walkthrough.title: When the Game Ends
walkthrough.text: The game continues around the table until someone places their last settlement. The round finishes so everyone has equal turns. Then you score: evaluate each of the 3 Kingdom Builder cards for all players, add 3 gold per Castle, and the highest total wins. In our practice game, Red likely wins thanks to early location tiles and good spread. Green is a solid second with Castles and Fishermen points. Blue's score suffers from being clumped in one area.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have just walked through the core flow of Kingdom Builder. You have seen how the adjacency rule controls your building, how location tiles amplify your turns, and how the 3 Kingdom Builder cards shape your strategy. You watched Blue learn the hard way that building in the center with no plan leads to a clumped, low-scoring kingdom. You are ready to play a full game on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Build
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every location tile, terrain type, and Kingdom Builder card.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Build
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix for quick-reference on all tiles and cards
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Let's start with the mistakes that sink new players. First, building in the middle of the board with no plan. Your settlements end up touching every terrain type and the adjacency rule clumps you into one blob. Second, ignoring location tiles. Players who skip locations get 3 placements per turn while opponents with tiles get 4 or 5. Third, forgetting the adjacency rule and having to take back settlements. It happens every first game. Fourth, not reading the Kingdom Builder cards before your first turn. Those cards define what earns gold. If you do not know what scores, you cannot build strategically.

walkthrough.title: Always Grab Location Tiles Early
walkthrough.text: This is the single most impactful tip in Kingdom Builder. On your first turn, look at the board and find a way to build next to a Location hex. Getting a location tile on turn 1 means you have an extra action every turn for the rest of the game. Over the course of a game, that could be 10 or more bonus settlements. A player with 2 location tiles is placing 5 settlements per turn while someone with none is placing 3. That gap is insurmountable. Make location tiles your first priority every game.

walkthrough.title: Minimize Adjacent Terrain Types
walkthrough.text: The adjacency rule is the biggest constraint in the game. If your settlements are adjacent to 4 different terrain types, nearly every card you draw will force you to build next to your cluster. If your settlements are adjacent to only 1 or 2 terrain types, you will draw cards for other terrain types and be free to start new clusters elsewhere on the board. Early in the game, try to place settlements so they border as few different terrain types as possible. Build in corners, along edges, or next to non-buildable terrain like Water and Mountains to limit adjacency.

walkthrough.title: Read the Kingdom Builder Cards Before Turn 1
walkthrough.text: Before you place a single settlement, study the 3 Kingdom Builder cards. They tell you exactly what earns gold. If Fishermen is in play, building near Water is a priority. If Lords is in play, you want settlements in as many board sections as possible. If Hermits is in play, you want many separate settlement clusters. Every card changes your priorities. Some cards even contradict each other, which is where the interesting decisions happen. Plan your first turn around the scoring conditions.

walkthrough.title: Spread Across the Board
walkthrough.text: Most Kingdom Builder cards reward having settlements in many different areas. Fishermen wants you near multiple Water hexes across the board. Knights wants a long horizontal line. Lords wants presence in all 4 board sections. Farmers wants settlements in all 4 quadrants. Clumping defeats nearly every scoring card. The adjacency rule will try to clump you, so actively fight it by limiting terrain borders and using location tiles like Paddock and Barn to reposition. The best players end the game with settlements scattered across the board, not piled in one corner.

walkthrough.title: Castles Are Free Points
walkthrough.text: Every game, regardless of which Kingdom Builder cards are in play, you score 3 gold for each Castle hex adjacent to your settlements. Castles are non-buildable terrain, so they help limit your adjacency footprint too. Whenever you can build near a Castle without compromising your main strategy, do it. Three gold per Castle adds up. A player adjacent to 4 Castles earns 12 gold just from that. Do not go out of your way to reach distant Castles, but never ignore one that is close.

walkthrough.title: Use Location Tiles Aggressively
walkthrough.text: Once you have location tiles, use them every single turn. It is easy to forget, especially when you are focused on your mandatory placements. Get in the habit of checking your tiles before and after your mandatory action. Some combinations are particularly strong. Oasis plus Farm gives you 2 bonus settlements per turn. Paddock lets you leap over obstacles to claim Castles or start new clusters. Barn lets you reposition a poorly placed settlement. Harbor puts settlements on Water for Fishermen scoring. Think of your tiles as superpowers and use them every turn.

walkthrough.title: Watch Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Kingdom Builder is not just solitaire. Watch where opponents build. If two players are competing for the same area near Water, one of them will run out of hexes. Location tiles are limited: only 2 per location hex, so grab them before opponents do. If Knights is in play, block your opponent's horizontal line by building settlements in their path. If an opponent is about to reach a Castle, consider whether you can get there first. You do not have many ways to directly interact, but positioning and timing matter. Stay aware of what everyone else is doing.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't build in the center with no plan — adjacency rule will clump you
Don't skip location tiles — extra actions compound all game
Always check adjacency rule before placing each settlement
Read the Kingdom Builder cards before your first turn

2 tiles = 5 placements per turn vs 3 without — gap is insurmountable
Make location tiles your first priority every game

summary.title: Minimize Adjacent Terrain Types
bullets: Fewer adjacent terrain types = more freedom on future turns
Build in corners, along edges, next to Water/Mountains to limit adjacency
Touch only 1-2 terrain types early so most cards free you to build elsewhere
This is the key to spreading across the board

summary.title: Read the Kingdom Builder Cards Before Turn 1
bullets: The 3 cards define how you earn gold — study them first
Fishermen: near Water. Lords: all board sections. Hermits: many clusters
Some cards contradict each other — that's where strategy lives
Plan your first turn around the scoring conditions

summary.title: Spread Across the Board
bullets: Most Kingdom Builder cards reward board-wide presence
The adjacency rule tries to clump you — actively fight it
Use Paddock and Barn to reposition and break out of clusters
Best players end with settlements scattered, not piled in one corner

summary.title: Castles Are Free Points
bullets: 3 gold per adjacent Castle, always, every game
Castles are non-buildable so they help limit adjacency footprint
A player adjacent to 4 Castles earns 12 gold from that alone
Never ignore a nearby Castle — don't go far out of your way either

bullets: Use every tile every turn — don't forget to check before and after mandatory action
Strong combos: Oasis + Farm = 2 bonus settlements per turn
Paddock leaps obstacles; Barn repositions; Harbor reaches Water
Think of tiles as superpowers — use them every turn

summary.title: Watch Your Opponents
bullets: Location tiles are limited (2 per hex) — grab them first
Block opponents' horizontal lines if Knights is in play
Watch for competition over Water hexes and Castle hexes
Positioning and timing matter even in this low-interaction game

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board at Setup
walkthrough.text: Before your first turn, spend a minute analyzing the board. Identify where the Location hexes are, which Castles are near buildable terrain, and where the Water runs. Look at the 3 Kingdom Builder cards and mentally rank the board sections from best to worst for this game's scoring. If Fishermen is in play, the section with the most accessible Water is gold. If Knights is in play, find the longest horizontal band of buildable terrain. Your opening move should target the intersection of the best location tile and the best scoring opportunity.

walkthrough.title: The Isolation Opening
walkthrough.text: Advanced players use what's called an isolation opening. On your first turn, you place your 3 settlements so they touch as few different terrain types as possible. Ideally, your settlements are adjacent to only 1 or 2 terrain types. This maximizes the number of future terrain cards that will not force you to build adjacent. When you draw a terrain type that is not adjacent to your settlements, you get to place anywhere on the board. This is how you start multiple separate clusters and cover the board for scoring cards that reward spread.

walkthrough.title: Location Tile Priority Order
walkthrough.text: Not all location tiles are equal, and their value changes with the Kingdom Builder cards in play. In general, the most universally powerful tiles are Paddock and Barn because they let you move settlements, breaking the adjacency constraint. Oracle is strong because it gives a free extra placement on your terrain type. Farm and Oasis are terrain-locked but still valuable for their extra placement. Tower is situational, great if there are Castles or Water at the edge. Tavern requires setup with long lines but pairs well with Knights. Harbor is powerful when Fishermen is in play. Prioritize tiles that counter the adjacency rule or directly support your scoring cards.

walkthrough.title: Using Paddock for Maximum Effect
walkthrough.text: The Paddock is arguably the strongest location tile in the game because it is the only action that ignores the adjacency rule. Jumping 2 hexes in a straight line lets you leap over mountains, water, and other settlements to reach isolated areas of the board. Use Paddock to claim distant Castles, start new clusters for Hermits or Lords, or extend horizontal lines for Knights. The key insight is that Paddock settlements do not need to be adjacent to anything, so they give you a foothold in areas you would never reach through normal building.

walkthrough.title: Card-Specific Strategy: Fishermen
walkthrough.text: Fishermen awards 1 gold for each settlement adjacent to a Water hex. Water hexes are scattered across the board, so this card rewards spreading out along coastlines. The adjacency rule helps here because Water is non-buildable, so building near it does not add to your terrain type count. Prioritize Harbor tiles if available, since moving settlements onto Water hexes gets them adjacent to even more Water (and other scoring hexes). Remember: settlements on Water hexes do not score for Fishermen, only settlements adjacent to Water.

walkthrough.title: Card-Specific Strategy: Knights
walkthrough.text: Knights awards 2 gold per settlement in a horizontal line. Only your longest horizontal line counts. This means you want one very long east-west chain, not multiple short ones. Tavern tiles are incredibly strong here because they let you extend a line of 3 into a line of 4, then 5, and so on. Look for the horizontal band across the board with the most buildable terrain and fewest gaps. Mountains and Water break lines, so route around them. In a Knights game, every settlement should ideally contribute to one massive horizontal line.

walkthrough.title: Card-Specific Strategy: Spread Cards (Lords, Hermits, Farmers)
walkthrough.text: Lords awards gold for settlements in all 4 board sections. Hermits awards gold per separate settlement cluster. Farmers awards gold based on your fewest settlements in any one quadrant. All three reward spreading across the board rather than clumping. Against these cards, the isolation opening is essential. You want to start small clusters in many areas early, then grow them. Paddock and Barn are your best tiles because they let you jump to new sections. Be aggressive about limiting terrain adjacency to give yourself free turns to build in new areas.

walkthrough.title: Card-Specific Strategy: Scoring Per Settlement Area
walkthrough.text: Citizens awards gold based on the size of your largest settlement cluster. Miners awards gold based on settlements adjacent to mountains. Discoverers awards gold based on the number of horizontal lines on which you have at least one settlement. Each of these has a distinct optimal shape. Citizens wants one mega-cluster, which contradicts the usual spread strategy. Miners wants settlements along mountain ranges. Discoverers wants at least 1 settlement on every horizontal row of the board. When Citizens is paired with a spread card like Hermits, you face a genuine strategic tension where you must decide which card to prioritize.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Settlement Depletion
walkthrough.text: The game ends when someone runs out of settlements. With 40 settlements and 3 per turn plus extra actions, the game typically lasts 8 to 12 rounds. If you have more location tiles than your opponents, you will run out of settlements faster. This can be an advantage because you control when the game ends. If you are ahead, deplete your supply quickly to end the game before opponents catch up. If you are behind, slow down by skipping optional extra actions. Timing the end of the game is an advanced tool that many players overlook.

walkthrough.title: Opponent Denial and Blocking
walkthrough.text: Kingdom Builder has limited direct interaction, but denial plays are powerful at higher levels. If you see an opponent building toward a location tile, race to claim it first since only 2 tiles exist per hex. If Knights is in play, place a settlement in the path of an opponent's horizontal line to break it. If an opponent needs to reach a specific Castle, fill the adjacent hexes before they can. Barn and Paddock are strong defensive tools because they let you place settlements in unexpected positions. The best Kingdom Builder players balance their own scoring with targeted disruption of the leader.

entries.definition: One of the 5 buildable terrain types. Shown as green open grassland hexes. Settlements can be built here when a Grass terrain card is played, or via the Farm location tile.
One of the 5 buildable terrain types. Shown as brown rocky canyon hexes. Settlements can be built here when a Canyon terrain card is played.
One of the 5 buildable terrain types. Shown as yellow sandy hexes. Settlements can be built here when a Desert terrain card is played, or via the Oasis location tile.
One of the 5 buildable terrain types. Shown as colorful floral hexes. Settlements can be built here when a Flower Field terrain card is played.
One of the 5 buildable terrain types. Shown as dark green tree-covered hexes. Settlements can be built here when a Forest terrain card is played.

A non-buildable terrain type. You cannot place settlements on Castle hexes. At end of game, each Castle hex adjacent to at least one of your settlements earns you 3 gold. Multiple settlements adjacent to the same Castle still only earn 3 gold.

A non-buildable terrain type. You cannot place settlements on Water hexes through normal building. The only way to place a settlement on Water is via the Harbor location tile. Settlements adjacent to Water score for the Fishermen Kingdom Builder card.

A non-buildable terrain type. You cannot place settlements on Mountain hexes. Mountains act as natural barriers on the board. Settlements adjacent to Mountains score for the Miners Kingdom Builder card.

A non-buildable hex on the game board that holds location tiles. Each board section has 2 matching location hexes. Building a settlement adjacent to a Location hex lets you claim one of its tiles. You cannot build on the Location hex itself.

A wooden house-shaped piece in a player's color. Each player has 40 settlements. Settlements are placed on buildable terrain hexes during the game. The game ends when any player places their last settlement.

One of 25 cards showing a buildable terrain type (5 each of Grass, Flower Field, Forest, Canyon, Desert). Players hold exactly 1 card at a time. The card dictates which terrain type you must build on during your mandatory action.

One of 10 scoring condition cards. Three are randomly selected each game and placed face up. They define how players earn gold at game end, in addition to the 3 gold per adjacent Castle. Different combinations create different strategic priorities each game.
A scoring marker in each player's color. Placed on the gold score track (the back side of an unused board section) to record final scores at game end.
Given to the oldest player. Indicates who goes first. Play proceeds clockwise. The game round finishes when the player to the right of the start player completes their turn.

A double-sided tile placed on Location hexes during setup. When a player builds adjacent to a Location hex, they claim the tile. Each tile grants one extra action per turn. There are 8 types: Oracle, Farm, Oasis, Tower, Tavern, Barn, Harbor, and Paddock.
The back side of an unused game board section. Used to record final scores. Each player places their gold marker on the black starting space during setup and advances it during final scoring.
Reference cards showing the pictogram and effect of each location tile type. Placed next to the corresponding board section during setup to remind players what each location tile does.

Location tile (extra action). Build one additional settlement on a hex of the same terrain type as your played terrain card. The adjacency rule applies: build adjacent to your own settlements if possible. Functions like a bonus 4th mandatory placement.

Location tile (extra action). Build one additional settlement on a Grass hex, regardless of your played terrain card. The adjacency rule applies. Skip this action if no unoccupied Grass hexes remain on the board.

Location tile (extra action). Build one additional settlement on a Desert hex, regardless of your played terrain card. The adjacency rule applies. Skip this action if no unoccupied Desert hexes remain on the board.

Location tile (extra action). Build one additional settlement on any buildable terrain hex at the edge of the game board. The hex must be one of the 5 buildable types and on the outer rim. The adjacency rule applies.

Location tile (extra action). Build one additional settlement at one end of a straight line of at least 3 of your own settlements. The line can be horizontal or diagonal. The target hex must be a buildable terrain type. Very strong with Knights scoring.

Location tile (extra action — move). Move one of your existing settlements to a hex of the same terrain type as your played terrain card. The adjacency rule applies: move adjacent if possible. A repositioning tool for correcting poor placements.

Location tile (extra action — move). Move one of your existing settlements to a Water hex. This is the only way to build on Water in the entire game. The adjacency rule applies. Note: Fishermen does not award gold for settlements on Water hexes.

Location tile (extra action — move). Move one of your existing settlements exactly 2 hexes in a straight line in any direction (horizontal or diagonal). You may jump over any terrain or settlements. The target hex must be unoccupied and buildable. Crucially, the adjacency rule does NOT apply to the Paddock — the only exception in the game.
The core action of each turn: build exactly 3 settlements from your personal supply onto unoccupied hexes matching your played terrain card. Must follow all building rules. Cannot be skipped.

A bonus action granted by a location tile. Each tile can be used once per turn. Extra actions may be performed before, after, or between mandatory settlements. Flip the tile to its picture side after use to track usage.

The most important building rule: each new settlement must be built adjacent to at least one of your existing settlements, if any adjacent hex of the required terrain type is available. Only when no adjacent matching hex exists can you build elsewhere. Applies to all actions except the Paddock.

Three core rules that apply to every settlement placement: (1) One settlement per hex. (2) Only buildable terrain types (Grass, Canyon, Desert, Flower Field, Forest). (3) Build adjacent to your own settlements whenever possible.

One of 8 double-sided boards. The front shows a landscape of 100 terrain hexes with 2 location hexes. The back shows the gold score track. Four sections are assembled into the game board; one extra is used for the score track.

Kingdom Builder card. Earn 1 gold for each of your settlements built adjacent to at least one Water hex. Settlements on Water hexes (placed via Harbor) do not count. Rewards spreading along coastlines.
Kingdom Builder card. Earn 2 gold for each settlement in your longest horizontal line of connected settlements. Only one line counts (your longest). Rewards building one massive east-west chain.

Kingdom Builder card. Earn 4 gold for each Location hex that is connected to at least one of your settlements. Connection means adjacent to your settlement or connected through a chain of adjacent settlements.
Kingdom Builder card. Earn 12 gold for having at least one settlement in each of the 4 board sections, 6 gold for 3 sections, and smaller amounts for fewer. Rewards spreading across the entire board.

Kingdom Builder card. Earn 1 gold for each separate settlement area (cluster) you have. A settlement area is a group of your adjacent settlements. More isolated clusters means more gold. Rewards spreading out with many disconnected groups.

Kingdom Builder card. Earn 1 gold for each settlement in your largest settlement area (cluster). Rewards building one big connected group rather than spreading out. Can create tension with spread-focused cards.
Kingdom Builder card. Earn 1 gold for each of your settlements built adjacent to at least one Mountain hex. Rewards building along mountain ranges.

Kingdom Builder card. Earn 3 gold for each settlement in the sector (quadrant) where you have the fewest settlements. You must have at least 1 settlement in every sector to qualify. The board is divided into 4 sectors by a horizontal and vertical center line. Rewards balanced placement across all quadrants.

Kingdom Builder card. Earn 1 gold for each horizontal line on the game board on which you have built at least one settlement. Rewards having presence on as many horizontal rows as possible across the entire board.
Kingdom Builder card. Earn 1 gold for each of your settlements adjacent to a Location hex or a Castle hex. Rewards building near special non-buildable hexes.

A cluster of adjacent settlements belonging to one player. Used for scoring Citizens (largest area) and Hermits (number of areas). Two settlements are in the same area if they are connected through a chain of adjacent hexes occupied by that player.

The game board is divided into 4 quadrants (sectors) by a horizontal and vertical center line. Used for scoring the Farmers Kingdom Builder card, which rewards balanced placement across all 4 sectors.

A row of hexes running east-west across the game board. Used for scoring Knights (longest horizontal line of your settlements) and Discoverers (number of horizontal lines with at least one of your settlements).

In the hex grid, a hex is adjacent to the 6 hexes directly surrounding it. Adjacency is used for the building rule, claiming location tiles, Castle scoring, and several Kingdom Builder card scoring conditions.

A player's stock of unplaced settlements. Each player starts with 40. Settlements leave the supply when built and return if moved (the moved settlement is relocated, not returned). The game ends when any player's supply is empty.
The outer rim of hexes on the assembled 4-section board. Used by the Tower location tile, which can place a settlement on any buildable edge hex. Edge hexes have fewer than 6 neighbors.

Played terrain cards go here after each turn. When the draw pile is exhausted, shuffle the discard pile to form a new draw pile. Terrain cards removed from the game due to having no valid hex are not placed in the discard pile.
